speaker: interviewer
text: The date is November 13th, 1996. Rescuer is Madeleine Morris, maiden name Jeantet. Interviewer, Naomi Rappaport. The city is New York, the state New York, the country USA, the language English. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My name is Naomi Rappaport, N-A-O-M-I R-A-P-P-A-P-O-R-T. And today is November the 13th, 1996. I am conducting an interview with Dr. Madeleine Morris in New York, New York, USA. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Good afternoon, Dr. Morris, and thank you very much for welcoming us to your home and sharing your experiences with us during the war. I'd like to start with your name. Could you give us your full name, and spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My first name is Madeleine, M-A-D-E-L-E-I-N-E. My middle initial is Fields. And my last name is Morris, M-O-R-R-I-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell Fields?

speaker: interviewee
text: F-I-E-L-D-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name at birth was Jeantet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My maiden name. J-E-A-N-T-E-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Except when I was a child, I was called Mado, M-A-D-O. But it was a child name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: February 5th, 1923.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age-- your present age?

speaker: interviewer
text: And in what city were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Bellegarde, which is a border town near Geneva, Switzerland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell Bellegarde?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-E-L-L-E-G-A-R-D-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: J-A?

speaker: interviewee
text: Garde.

speaker: interviewer
text: Garde.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: J-A-R-D-E?

speaker: interviewee
text: G-A-R-D-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: G-A-R-D-E. And in what country was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In France.

speaker: interviewer
text: In France. Thank you very much. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You said that Bellegarde was near Geneva.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large was Bellegarde?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bellegarde was mostly an industrial town, half industrial and half farmers, cheese maker, diamond cutters, stonecutters, watchmakers. Typical of the Alps, even though Bellegarde is just at the beginning of the Alps, between the Jura mountains and the Alps.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how big? How many people lived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time, about 5,000. Now probably around 50,000. Now it's a rather large place. And we have a street to our name, because my uncle was the mayor for over 30 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had your family lived there for a long time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Has been here, there, or around, you know, in that part of France, since-- since a long time, probably since the Reformation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your father and mother do you in Bellegarde?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a pharmacist, and my mother was in the teaching profession.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where-- do you remember where you lived in Bellegarde? What kind of street and house you lived in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why, yes, I-- I lived in what you call [FRENCH], which is the school building. In France, the teachers-- my mother was a teacher there-- live in the building where the school is. But I don't have particular memories. I mean, as a child I didn't pay too much attention.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it look? Have you any idea? Do you remember at all what it looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I remember the wallpaper of the kitchen, that kind of thing. But it is absolutely of no importance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a big apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was large, and it was large, and we did have a maid and all this, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was provided by the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: By my parents, because I was small and my mother was teaching, so we needed someone to watch me and my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the house was part of the job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother's job. And your father's pharmacy?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father lived there too, but he was working in Geneva at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your father have a pharmacy in Bellegarde as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not a pharmacy. It was-- it was a plant where you make pharmaceutical products. Like would be Merck or Johnson, or that kind of thing. And he was a pharmacist there. And I understand-- I don't remember, but I understand that the plant burned down. And my father found another position in Dijon, and that's when we moved to Dijon. And my mother moved there too, me too, and my sister. And we chose Dijon because the school system was particularly good. And that's where I lived. And I lived there until-- until I left for America.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far is Dijon from Bellegarde?

speaker: interviewee
text: To us it was far, because of the transportation then. Now it's about two hours by-- by car, and it's not far from Lausanne, it's not far from Switzerland, it's not far from-- from the east, you see. It's on the east part of France. It is the capital of Burgundy.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Bellegarde itself-- you went to school in Bellegarde, your mother taught there-- what was the community like? What was the ethnic distribution of the community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were mostly French, but we had a few Italian workers. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a Jewish community in Bellegarde?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Maybe there was, but I never heard of it. I never even heard what Jewish people were until much later.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your family life, your social life, your religious life in Bellegarde, I'm talking about.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was originally Catholic, but she had turned back-- turned her back on it. And my father was not church-going, but he was of Protestant background. My grandmother on my father's side was Swiss, and I think her brother was a missionary, but I never met them. They died when I was very young. But my grandfather was a very well-known activist on-- on the left of the middle, was much in politics, and-- and the way we all, usually, when people were teaching in the public schools and were of Protestant background, there were very what you'd call now liberal, which is a dirty word now, but it's still a wonderful word. And so he was always for the-- for the underdog, you could say.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we always have been raised to be for the underdog, because we were always reminded that our past as descendants of Protestants, we had been persecuted. We had been chased. We had been killed and burned in this process through the century until the Revolution.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that we had a Protestant diaspora, and that the French Protestants were all over the world, including the grandfather of George Washington, the grandfather of Martha Washington, the father of Paul Revere, and on, and on, and on. So we were very sensitive to refugee problem, mostly political refugees.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were-- was this information imparted to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: By my grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have your grandfather's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, his name was Jean. [LAUGHS] He didn't have much imagination. Jean Jeantet. John Johnson, you could say. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did he do this? Did he--

speaker: interviewee
text: All talks. My grandfather took a lot of time to talk to us, mostly about Republican ideals and mor-- morality. I mean, he was really a teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he live with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was-- he was a principal of that big-- big place. And I even met people who had been his students. I mean, a few years ago, remember him for being a very harsh and very judge-- very just person. But he was a-- he was really a believer in human rights, and a pacifist. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you consider your grandfather a role model for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have other role models in your family or in your environment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father and my mother were wonderful people. And my pastor. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But-- and of course, we are taught French history, you know. We were-- I remember being five years old and I went to Brittany, and I ate [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what you call sea food. And I got a very bad case of yellow jaundice, and I was very ill. And I was taken back home, and I remember feeling well one morning, and waking up, and feeling extremely happy. And the feeling remains with me to this day, and I was very happy for two reasons. The first one that was I was a girl and not a boy. Here goes for it. And the second thing that I was French. I thought with those two things, nobody could be happier than I was. And I've been living like that since. Being happy to be French and being happy to be a woman. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you treated differently as a young girl by your parents and your grandparents and by your male relatives.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't-- I didn't have any brothers. And I have a sister who is-- was a year younger, not quite a year younger. We were almost like twins, got along beautifully. No problems. Nice childhood. Nice school years. Everything very nice. Nice grandparents. Good vacation in the country in Switzerland, and all the nice things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you considered a wealthy family, a middle-class family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. And besides, we were always raised below our capacity. My father was an austere man, and we had a seamstress coming to the house for two weeks every summer, and she made our dresses and our underwear. Two dresses for the summer, two dresses for the winter, and that was that. We were raised in a very strict manner, and to the age of 20, as I had mentioned to you, I never went out after 7:00 in the af-- in the evening. I never went to a dance. I never learned to dance. I never played-- learned to play anything, cards or anything. I still don't know how to play anything. And never had a date.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: My friends were my girlfriends from school with whom I'm still friends-- the ones who are still alive, of course. We still see each other in the summer. They were raised pretty much the way I was. And for us, was accounting more than anything was our studies. We loved our school. We loved our studies.

speaker: interviewee
text: We learned things by heart that were not even requested. I know poems that never were requested that we learned together for-- for the love of it. And that served me well later on, you know? I almost passed my orals at the Piaget Columbia with all the things I had learned by heart with my friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we were serious students, and we loved our school and the teaching that was done there, which was difficult, but very, very nice. But we were totally apolitical. In the school, we didn't know anything but our world, our duty, our parents, the family, and the school. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did this continue when you moved to Dijon? Was it the same pattern?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, here-- oh, until I was married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go to Dijon a minute. Where did you live in Dijon?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in an apartment house, a very beautiful. Was one of the first great co-op in Dijon, on the best avenue in the best part of the city. And we had a nice apartment with a wonderful view of the country, the hills, the vineyards. Was beautiful. Now it's all built around. People who live where we used to live, they don't have the same thing. But it was very, very nice. Except that during the war, we had a collective heating system and we couldn't heat the house. And we had a concierge who watched everybody going in and out, and therefore you had very restricted freedom, you could say.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was an apartment house?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was an apartment house.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people lived in that whole building?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that apartment, there were about-- they were a large apartment, about 10 families.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but the way the French know each other. You know, they were like cats. They like to be nearby, but not too close. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- well, upper middle class or middle class. You know, they were people in-- in business, doctors. You know, people like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the ethnic distribution in the area that you were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: All catholic. We were-- there, everybody was Catholic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Except you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Except us. We were always a bit marginal, you could say.

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- how large is Dijon, or was it then?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time, it was about 80,000 people. Now it's about 200,000 because the suburbs and all. But the city itself-- it was the capital of Burgundy at the time of way, way back, you know? I think in the 10th century. And we have beautiful buildings with houses with the-- I don't know what you call this in English. You see the wood outside. You know, like in Alsace, like in-- in-- in England, or like in Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tudor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And the center of the city is very well preserved. It's very pretty. It's a very artistic town, not at all a commercial or a factory place. Everybody there was more or less a lawyer or very stiff-necked, very conservative, very secretive, extremely Catholic. Churches all over the place. You could hear it-- at night, you could hear the bells. You know, none of the clocks was on time, but it would take about 15 minutes for everything to ring. Was pretty. I liked it. But it is a very Catholic, very conservative town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father and mother were both Protestants?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was nothing. My father was Protestant in name only. Never went to the temple, as we call it there. But we had the freedom to choose. And me, I turned to the Protestant Church and remained.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a Protestant church in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of Protestant church?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Christian-- I mean, it was a Reform church. The Calvinist Church, the Church of Geneva, which is-- maybe you don't know, but it is at the basis of modern democracy. That's on the pattern of that church that the United States was conceived by the founding fathers. It is a place where you have no hierarchy, where everybody is equally minister, equally qualified. You have no bishop. You have nothing. Everybody is the same. It's a low church without candles, without anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your father attend that church as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he didn't, but he approved.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he go to any church there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He didn't go to any church. He went to church when I did get married, but otherwise, no. But me, I taught Sunday school, and I was extremely involved. I remain involved to this day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- was there a Jewish community in Dijon?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a synagogue? Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you familiar with it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know very much. There it was an [INAUDIBLE] extremely close community. They didn't let anybody in, at all. We knew they were Jewish if it was mentioned, but to me didn't mean anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know any Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The first time I-- I became aware of the question is in 1936. I was 13 years old and I was-- my-- my father and my grandmother coming from Basel, my father having been a prisoner in Germany so long during the First World War, and because we were half Swiss, half French and all, I learned German early. I didn't speak it very well because we spoke French at home.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father had the idea of taking me to Germany in 1936. So I went to Germany. It was the year of the Olympic Games. It was also the year of the occupation of the Saar, when they reoccupied the Rhineland and we didn't do anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the year of the war in-- the Civil War in Spain, where we had refugees coming from all the bombings, you know, of all-- you know Picasso and all the shooting, Guernica and all. And at the same time, we had a lot of strikes in France. France was a mess. It was when we had Leon Blum as president, and he was the one who-- who arranged and managed to have, for the French workers, the first time ever in history, vacation. The French worker never had a day of vacation-- paid vacation-- ever, ever. And it is Leon Blum who did that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it created a lot of problems. My father was very enthusiastic. So was I. But all the snobs said, here are they coming. We cannot go on vacation. All the Slavs are coming, messing up our vacation. You know, the usual, as you have that here with the blacks and-- OK. So it was the time when we went to Germany and my father enrolled me in a school, not long, couple months in the Rhineland. And on top of the door of the school was written, in beautiful Gothic letter-- maybe you know German? It was written [GERMAN], which means, anybody who buys from the Jews is a traitor to his people. And I went under that door and finally asked my dad, what does that mean? And he explained to me what the Jews were. I though the Jews were people in the Bible. You know? But I didn't know they existed now. And I-- And you were 13 then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 13. Then we went-- I went out with the [GERMAN], you know? They were playing the guitar, singing song, picnics, flowers everywhere. Order. Everything was clean. The old people were very respected, not like in the-- in the sub-- in the-- you know, in the bus, on Broadway, where all the kids take the seats and the older people stand. You know, kids here have absolutely no education whatsoever. I mean, it's shameful. But there, the old-- we had to give our seat to older people. To me, it was a paradise. Germany, 1936. Of course it was made for the Olympic Games with the sign of Wilkommen, Welcome, Bienvenue everywhere. Flowers. It was beautiful. You had no idea what was cooking up there. And I came back to-- to France with a fantastic impression. You know me was always kept with my school work and all that. There was sport, they were singing, they were outing. And me, I came back to my French gray life, all work and no fun at all. And I remember that interlude in Germany as being something marvelous. We went to Berlin, and then I don't know exactly. I recounted afterwards what we did there. As a matter of fact, my father had gone to make contact with-- with Jewish family. I didn't know. And those Jewish family were extremely German. For instance, because I was French and my father was French, they wanted to treat us well. They were very rich. They had servants. And they served us asparagus. Maybe it makes you smile, but at the time of Hitler, you just didn't grow asparagus because it was a wasteful food, a non-nourishing food.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the servant brought a dish of asparagus, and the kids, I mean, they were little neighbors or friends. The father-- Walter, the man of the door-- came with his big knife, and he cut the asparagus in two, and he give all the ends to the children and all the heads to the parents. I was so insulted that I didn't eat any of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, because in my house, not the children were given the best and first like in America where the kids first eat-- serve themselves, help themselves first like-- like pigs. No. We waited to be served. But we were served decently like the family. There we were given what we would have given to the dog in France. And I was really shocked. Then we came back to France and then started the-- the thing with Walter Tradelius. He was a pharmacist in a big plant like my father's. And he knew that something was going wrong. He had read So had my father. Me too, but, you know, as 13 years old, how can you make out But he arranged with my father that all the customers he had in South America, who ordered their pharmaceutical products, I mean, in mass from-- from Berlin, would receive a letter explaining in that they should direct all their orders to my father's place in Dijon. And that's what happened. We inherited all the customers from-- who were going to Berlin. They came to us in Dijon. So the business boomed, naturally. My father arranged for Walter Tradelius to come to Dijon first. Then he went to Berlin and had the furniture moved. Then he went back to get his wife and his children. And they all came to Dijon and lived in apartment not too far from us. And we became very friendly. They were people socially much richer than we are-- were. They had furniture you couldn't believe. I mean, glasses, everything you could dream of. But, you know, they had to put all this in a small apartment. They felt cramped. They didn't feel very happy. And Annalise, their daughter, was about three years older than I was, but she was put in my grade at school-- in public school-- to learn French. So she was sitting next to me, and me with my German, I could help her out. And so we became very good friends and we visited in each other's house for every week. And that was very nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he took me-- the father took me to pastry shops. You know I was-- well, what was I? 14, 15 years old? And to have-- on Thursday, which was the day off, you know? We have a day off in the-- in France in our schools for religious instruction, where everybody goes to their own church, and it's a day off. Now it's Wednesday. At the time it was Thursday. So Thursday afternoon he would take me to a pastry shop, and he took me-- he treated me like a little lady. He give me a doll, and he gave me my first bracelet. I don't have it anymore. It was made out of wood. It was plaques of wood with a little heart. You opened the door and you could put a picture in it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he treated me like a woman. You know, it was the first time it happened to me that a man really was really kind and nice with me. He was, you know, about the age of my dad and wanted to learn French with me. And of course he taught me a little bit more German. And I was-- I became very attached to him, naturally. And we were very, very good friends. And at the time we had a movement in France-- Catholic-- called the [FRENCH], the cross of fire, fire crosses. It was something like you could transpose, but very far, the Buchanan type of sort, except not so stupid. They were the upper class-- very, very Catholic, very nationalistic. And they started to-- to explain that all those German people who came-- first, they were Jews and they were unwanted. And the other thing, they were mixed with the fifth column. They came for spying. And you have to be careful. And to consult with them, it was to be anti-French. It was to be a traitor. So you-- you had to be careful. And I have to say that the German refugees-- in particular, the wife of Walter, who had been what you call in America a Jewish princess, who had been spoiled, who was kept precious, frivolous, covered with jewelry, met with her-- met with her friends in the cafes of France. You know how the cafes are. It's not like a bar here. It's outside and you-- you meet your friends and you talk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- they took over particularly the cafes of Dijon, and the French people felt, what are those people doing there? You know, they kind of-- the people didn't like them very much. They were too showy, too noisy, too much there. How many were there? How many of these Jews came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were quite a few, because they all came to Dijon-- I should have explained it before. Because the first who-- who migrated from Germany, they stopped in Alsace. Why? Because in Alsace, German was spoken. It was close in Germany. But then there was a law that all those refugees could not live so near the border. They had to live at least 200 miles inland. And that's how they ended up in Dijon. I don't know how many there were. They were maybe a dozen here, a dozen there. But they were speaking German, and they were very much there, having their coffee klatch and their pastries, not in the French manner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have much contact with them and their children? You personally.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, because they were old. I mean, they're people the age of my mother, and I didn't go to cafe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I suppose some had. I-- the Tradelius did. I mean, they had Annalise. And also at that time, something that has bothered me since. We had those big cafe houses that were quite elegant, where women met for tea. It was not a place for drinking, but it was a nice place to meet, like in Paris now. And they had orchestras. And those orchestras at the time were made up, I realized later, from those Jewish refugees. Maybe lawyers, doctors, people like that who were of excellent education, who were amateur, probably, playing the violin, the piano, and put together orchestra to survive. Because what they knew, whether in medicine or law, you couldn't use in France.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to have to change our tape now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to change our tape now.
